title: Fault-Tolerant QLDPC Syndrome Measurement via LDGM Encoding
abstract: We propose the use of certain low-density generator-matrix (LDGM) codes as syndrome measurement (SM) codes for quantum low-density parity check (QLDPC) codes. We use an efficient progressive-edge-growth-like algorithm to create LDGM SM codes with column and row weights that result in measured stabilizers that have constant weight, thus preserving the desirable properties of the underlying QLDPC code. This process allows for control over stabilizer weights and SM code distance, resulting in significantly better performance than repeated syndrome extraction and allowing for both higher distances and fewer syndrome measurements. We implement these SM codes on a distance-5 rotated surface code, and show that this procedure results in a lower probability of logical error. As syndrome measurements performed are a reasonable metric for the time a circuit takes to implement, we conclude that these LDGM codes allow for improved implementation of QLDPC codes without sacrificing the low weights of the syndrome measurements performed.
summary: The paper introduces a new method for syndrome measurement in QLDPC codes using LDGM encoding to keep stabilizer weights low and constant. This approach reduces the number of required measurements and prevents error propagation, leading to better fault-tolerant performance than traditional repeated extraction methods.
main_problem: How to perform fault-tolerant syndrome measurement in QLDPC codes without increasing stabilizer weights to unbounded levels, which would otherwise cause error propagation and destroy the code's low-weight advantages.
main_result: The use of LDGM-based syndrome measurement codes allows for controlled stabilizer weights and higher code distances, outperforming standard repeated syndrome extraction with fewer total measurements.
method: The authors propose using Low-Density Generator-Matrix (LDGM) codes as syndrome measurement (SM) codes, constructed via a progressive-edge-growth-like algorithm and graph lifting techniques.
model_or_system: The study focuses on Quantum Low-Density Parity-Check (QLDPC) codes, specifically using a [[25, 1, 5]] rotated surface code as an illustrative example, under a phenomenological error model involving both qubit and measurement errors.
key_observables: Logical error probability, stabilizer weight, effective code distance, and measurement efficiency factor.
important_parameters: Stabilizer weight (w), SM code distance (d_SM), measurement error probability (p_m), and the improvement factor (alpha) representing the ratio of measurements compared to repeated extraction.
main_assumptions: The assumption of constant-weight stabilizers to justify uniform measurement error probability, and the use of Shor-style syndrome extraction circuits.
figures_summary: Table I shows the mapping of a Hamming code to stabilizer elements; Figure 1 depicts the rotated surface code; Figure 2 shows the generator matrices for the LDGM codes; Figure 3 and 4 compare logical error probabilities under different error models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of weight inflation in QLDPC syndrome measurement, proposes an LDGM-based construction using PEG algorithms and graph lifting, presents numerical simulations using importance sampling to compare performance against repeated extraction, and discusses limitations regarding geometric locality.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: The distance-5 rotated surface code, illustration from [27]. White dots correspond to the 25 data qubits, which we number sequentially as 1 to 25 in lexicographic order, while gray dots correspond to the ancilla qubits which measure their corresponding stabilizers. Green squares correspond to Z-stabilizers while red squares correspond to X-stabilizers.

Fig. 2: Visualizations of the codes generated from the proto- graph matrices. White squares represent a 0 in the correspond- ing location, while black squares represent a 1. The red lines serve to divide the matrix into squares, where each square corresponds to an entry in the corresponding protograph. For example, the top left square in graph (a) corresponds to the entry x6 in H(2) 2×5, and shows the corresponding cyclic permutation. Similarly, in (c), the top-left square is the 4 × 4 identity matrix, which corresponds to the top-left entry 1 in the protograph.

Fig. 3: A comparison between the probability of logical error resulting from measurement error for the codes generated by the six different protographs, as well as a comparison to 5-fold repetition. This only considers errors manifesting as bit-flips on the measurements, and not qubit errors.

Fig. 4: A comparison between the probability of logical error resulting from measurement and qubit error for the codes generated by the six different protographs, as well as a comparison to 5-fold repetition. This assumes errors occur with probability pm on each stabilizer measurement, and qubit errors occur with probability pq = pm/5 on all qubits.
